meaning: occurs in wəˀətu 'he', ˀəmmuntu 'they', ˀəsƒəntu 'how much?', zəntu 'this', zəktu 'that'.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. taˀaka mängəst 'palace' (from Geez).
from Gk. theomakhos 'fighting against God'.
from Gk. theōriā 'theory'.

meaning: (T) tə-sit 'scum on top of molten metal, (Lt) mixture'.
'you say, you said, she says'; see bahla.
(Y.K) tabbəˀa 'be brave, be courageous, be like a man, be manly, (Y) be strong, be daring, be determined'
origin_explanation: (note that KG differentiates between tabˀa 'be manly', and tabbəˀa 'be strong, be determined'); ˀatbəˀa, causative; tatabˀa 'behave courageously'; tatabˀa 'prove to be courageous, encourage one another'; ˀastatabˀa, caus. of tatabˀa; tabˀi (pl. tabˀəyān) 'male, strong, determined'; tabbəˀa 'male, manly, masculine, headstrong, determined, courageous'; tabuˀ 'male, manly, strong'; tabˀ (Lt) 'vigor, courage'; təbˀ 'courage, bravery, virtue'; təbˀat 'courage, bravery, boldness, manliness, virtue'; tabˀət (pl. ˀatbuˀ) 'male, masculine; fort, rampart'; tabˀətāwi, tabˀətāy 'male, masculine'; təbˀənnā 'courage, manliness'; SEMITIC: perhaps Akk. tebû 'rise to make war', tebû 'insurgent'. Dillmann 561 connects the Geez root with Ar. tabia 'follow'. Hommel 56 is of the same opinion and attributes to Ar. tabia an original meaning 'unablässig sein Ziel verfolgen'. Bravmann 1977:375 explains G. tabˀi 'male' from Ar. tabiahu 'he followed him' > he served him as his man (in war)' and considers the meaning of G. tabˀa 'be strong' to be secondary. Conti Rossini 1931:256 (following Noeldeke) compares Ar. tabˀa 'complete, perfect'. Cerulli 1938:210, Brockelmann 1950:7, and Rundgren, OS 21 (1972). 56 rightly express doubt about the identification of G. tbˀ 'be strong' with Ar. tabia 'follow'. Reinisch 1895:188 connects Ethiopic tbˀ with Bed. raba, Sa.Af.Som. lab (also Bil. tab which is, however, borrowed from Tigre); for other Cushitic languages, see Cerulli 1938:210, under labbā. Brockelmann 1950:7 derives Ethiopic tbˀ from Cushitic (for the various Cushitic languages, see above). For Ar. tubbaˀ ('containing the

meaning: meaning of kings having followers', see Jeffery 89, Blachère 998.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. tåbä 'be courageous', tåb'at 'male, manly'. Tna. täb'e 'be courageous', täb'at 'male'. Amh.Gur. täbba 'be courageous', täbät 'male'. Gaf. täbba 'be abundant'. Gur. täbät 'male', passed into Cushitic. Bil. tab 'manly' (from Tigre), tabe 'be strong'.

meaning: 'mantle, mantle of priests, shoulder piece' (wrongly placed in Dillmann 560 after täbot); it translates, among others, the Heb. 'epod.
(Lt) tabsilä 'cooked dish, cooked vegetables, porridge'. 'atabsala (T.Y) 'make cook' (denominative); from Jud.Aram. tabšil (Noeldeke 1910:45); see also basala.
origin_explanation: 'atabsala (T.Y) 'make cook' (denominative); from Jud.Aram. tabšil (Noeldeke 1910:45); see also basala.

meaning: 'ark of Noah, ark of the Covenant, altar (by extension), case, coffer, slab on which the Ten Commandments are inscribed'. It is a coffer of gold and gems preserved in the cathedral of Aksum, containing a slab on which are inscribed the Ten Commandments, supposed to be the Ark of the Covenant carried from Jerusalem to Ethiopia by Menelik. In an other church it is a small oblong consecrated board of wood placed upon the slab of the altar (see Brightman 570, 589; David Buxton, The Abyssinians [1970], pp. 160-162, plates 100-103; Hammerschmidt 1962:215-17; Heyer 39ff; manbara täbot, lit. 'the throne or seat on which the täbot is placed'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. tebå 'chest' considered to be of Egyptian (dbjt) origin (Buhl 869). Aram. tebûtå, täbûtå 'be abundant'. Gur. täbät 'male'. Gaf. täbba 'be abundant'. Gur. täbät 'male', passed into Cushitic. Bil. tab 'manly' (from Tigre), tabe 'be strong'. On Ar. täbût 'ark' being either of Geez or Aramaic origin, see Noeldeke 1910:49, Jeffery 88, A. Fisch 1953:16; for Ar. täbût taken over through the intermediary of Judeo-Aramaic of the Medina Jews, M. Rodinson, GLECS 9 (1960-63):64ff. See Hammerschmidt 1967:127, Ullendorff 1968:82-9. It is doubtful whether Syr. qebûtå 'ark' is connected with tebûtå as suggested by Polotsky 6 (followed by Ullendorff 1968:122, n. 3). For qebûtå, see Brockelmann 1928:645. ETHIOPIC: Te. Tna.Amh.Gur. täbot.

meaning: (T.Lt), name of God, cross; tadåho (T), name of God; might represent the first letter of the Greek word theos 'God'. Or could it be a mystical representation of the name of God? For the names of the letter being a mystical representation of the names of God, cp. zäy 'name of a Geez letter' and 'name of God', mem 'name of a letter' and 'name of God'. See also Guidi 1906:921, and tä'os, above.
origin_explanation: might represent the first letter of the Greek word theos 'God'. Or could it be a mystical representation of the name of God? For the names of the letter being a mystical representation of the names of God, cp. zäy 'name of a Geez letter' and 'name of God', mem 'name of a letter' and 'name of God'. See also Guidi 1906:921, and tä'os, above.

meaning: (KG.K) 'submission, service, thanks'. probably transcription of Heb. tôdå.
origin_explanation: probably transcription of Heb. tôdå.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. dabit (with metathesis).

meaning: (T) 'sacristy, vestry'. is probably connected with tadåho. For the parts of the sanctuary ending in -hi, -hi, see Guidi 1906:921.
origin_explanation: is probably connected with tadåho. For the parts of the sanctuary ending in -hi, -hi, see Guidi 1906:921.

meaning: (KG) taf'a 'spit, spit out'; 'atfä'a (K+), causative; tafu, pass. participle; taffa 'spittle'; taf'at 'spittle, saliva'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. taffa (tff) 'spit'. Soq. tebih (with alternance of labials). Heb. tope(') 'spittle'. Aram. pap. tape 'spit', tûp 'spittle'. Barth 1893:28 connects it with Syr. tayyåbå 'spittle', as against Fraenkel 1898:74. ETHIOPIC: Te. täf'a 'spit', also täf'a; Tna. täf'e, täf'a bälä, also tuff bälä; Amh. täffa, 'ätäff bälä, Gaf. täffa. Har. tuf bäya, Arg. 'äntaf ala, Gur. täfa'. The root is commom with Cushitic: Or. tufe, Bil. tufo. Som. tuf. Sid. tifi. For this root used in blessings in the various Ethiopian languages, see Leslau 1979: 422, 592.

meaning: (D.Sw. in D 573), taffäh (D.K), taffäh (L), taffuh (T.K) tuffäh (T) 'apple, (K) potato, (O) banana, perfume' (for 'perfume', cp. Heb. tappu^h, below); from Ar. tuffäh 'apple'; also in Te. tofah, Amh. tufah; in the other Semitic languages: Heb. tappu^h 'apple, fragrant fruit'. For a bibliography dealing with the origin of the noun, see Buhl 886. See also täfäb, below.
origin_explanation: from Ar. tuffäh 'apple'; also in Te. tofah, Amh. tufah; in the other Semitic languages: Heb. tappu^h 'apple, fragrant fruit'. For a bibliography dealing with the origin of the noun, see Buhl 886. See also täfäb, below.

meaning: (Gr.201) 'trust, rely upon, (L.T) write'. originally 'trust > give one's consent in writing > write'.
origin_explanation: probably a secondary formation from tawfaya 'be entrusted, or handed over', for which see wafaya below (so Dillmann 573). On secondary verbs in Arabic formed by agglutinating the derivational morphemes, see A. Mez in Noeldeke 1906:249-254.

meaning: (T) = Amh. dägar 'a pair of wooden fittings attached to the shaft of the plough'.

(T) tagha 'be watchful, be vigilant, keep watch, be on the watch, keep vigil, be wakeful, exert oneself, apply oneself, be diligent, be eager, be attentive'; 'atgäha, causative; also 'wake up' (tr); tagähi, act. part. of tagha.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. tägha 'be diligent, be awake'. Tna. täghe, Amh. tägga, Gur. täga 'diligent'.

meaning: 'watchful, watcher (also referring to angels), wakeful, attentive, vigilant, diligent'; taguha 'watchfully'; tagäh 'vigilance, night vigil, watchfulness'; tagäh 'watching, vigil, vigilance, watchful care, watchfulness, diligence, application, eagerness, exactitude, promptness'; taghat 'being (n) awake, watch, diligence, zeal'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. tägha 'be diligent, be awake'. Tna. täghe, Amh. tägga, Gur. täga 'diligent'.

meaning: (T) 'iron tripod'; to be indentified with tegan I (see below), with alternance of liquids l.n.

'frying pan, baking pan, caldron, (T.Gr.429) earthenware tripod on which the cooking pot rests'; tegana (T) 'set up the hearthstones around the fire' (denominative); (M. 283 erroneously rendered Tayyä's Amh. g'ällätä (translation of tegana) by 'creó un feudo a beneficio ecclesiastico' (denominative of gult 'fief, land given as an endowment'), but in fact Amh. g'ällätä here is a denominative of Amh. gulläcäa 'tripod'; tigun, (K*) tegun 'fried in a pan'; tegnu (T.M) 'earthenware tripod'; tegun (T) 'iron griddle'.
origin_explanation: a secondary formation of tegan, below; see also tegal.

meaning: (T) 'ray of sun'; could it be a figurative meaning of tegan 'tripod', the hearthstones being regarded as rays?
origin_explanation: could it be a figurative meaning of tegan 'tripod', the hearthstones being regarded as rays?

meaning: (K*) 'trade, engage in commerce'; tagäri, tägre (K*) 'merchant'; from Ar. tagara 'trade'; also in Te. (tä)täggära 'become rich', Amh. taggära 'become a merchant, get rich'. For the identification of 'merchant:be come rich', cp. Sh. tegar 'become rich', from Gir 'be a merchant'. Ar. (Dat) tägir 'merchant, rich'. For this root in Arabic, see Blachère 1003-4. For Ar. tägir 'merchant, wine merchant', coming from Aram.-Syr. taggärå, see Fraenkel 181. Zimmern 16 considers Aram. taggärå to be of Akkadian origin, but see Salonen 11.
origin_explanation: from Ar. tagara 'trade'; also in Te. (tä)täggära 'become rich', Amh. taggära 'become a merchant, get rich'. For the identification of 'merchant:be come rich', cp. Sh. tegar 'become rich', from Gir 'be a merchant'. Ar. (Dat) tägir 'merchant, rich'. For this root in Arabic, see Blachère 1003-4. For Ar. tägir 'merchant, wine merchant', coming from Aram.-Syr. taggärå, see Fraenkel 181. Zimmern 16 considers Aram. taggärå to be of Akkadian origin, but see Salonen 11.

meaning: 'pitch a tent, pull out (the pegs of the tent), pull tight, stretch tight'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Heb. toḥōlā 'error, folly' (Dillmann 552), connected with Ar. waḥila 'err' (so also Buhl 871), Koehler 109). ETHIOPIC: perhaps metathesis of Tna. Amh. gāṭṭarā 'pull tight, stretch tight', Te. gāṭṭarā 'bend the bow'.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. tağraṭīya 'malicious spirit', Amh. tağreṭ, tağri 'malicious spirit, mild insanity (due to a spirit)', tağrīda, tağrīd 'epilepsy (manifested by someone possessed by a spirit)'. On tağraṭīya, see Mercier 274ff.
None
None

meaning: (T.D 1403) 'small chamber, cell, door or window opening'
origin_explanation: From Gk. thee, transcription of Heb. tā(ʾ).
None
None

meaning: 'breast, cow's udders swollen because of an abundance of milk, the meat of the breast'.
origin_explanation: None
from Heb. tebet, name of the tenth month.
None

meaning: 'dry grass, couch grass (Amh. särdo), weed, grass land'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. taḥag 'dry grass, grass land'; also in Cushitic: Sa. taḥag.

meaning: 'wander about restlessly, wander from place to place, be restless, be a wanderer, ramble, be incoherent, (Y) become weak'
origin_explanation: None

SEMITIC: Heb. toḥōlā 'error, folly' (Dillmann 552), connected with Ar. waḥila 'err' (so also Buhl 871), Koehler 109). ETHIOPIC: Amh. täḥlālā 'be lost in thought' < ṭhl, with reduplicated last radical.
None
None
None
None

SEMITIC: At. taḥt 'the lower part', taḥta 'under'. SAR. tht, Heb. taḥat, Aram. taḥōt, Syr. taḥūj, Md. tit, atuṭia, Ug. tht. ETHIOPIC: Tna. täḥata 'be low', Te. täḥat 'the lower part', Amh. (ṭä)taṭā 'be humble', täḥut 'humble lowly' (from Geez), tačč 'down there, below', Gu. tačča, Har. taḥay, Gur. tat.

ETHIOPIC: Te. täḥsas, Tna. taḥsas, Amh. taḥsas, tasas, Gur. täsas; passed into Cushitic: Tem. täsäsa. Had. täsäsa. Tigrinya täḥšāš 'the harvest is finished' could either be the origin of G. täḥšāš or a denominative. Conti Rossini 1923:376 derives G. täḥšāš, with reservations, from ḥasāšā 'search', that is, 'il mese in cui la scorrería muove alla ricerca del bottino'. Cp. also Cushitic: Bil. ḥasas 'harvest'.
None

meaning: 'plant, fix, fasten, implant, drive in, set up, establish, pitch (a tent), drive a stake into the ground'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Noeldeke, ZDMG 40 (1886), 726 asks whether the original meaning of the root tkl is that of Geez 'plant, fix' > 'have confidence, rely on' as in Syr. takel, Akk. takkālu. However, Syr. (also Aram.Md), Akk. tkl could go back to wkl, as in Ar. 8th form ittakala 'reply, depend' forming a second ary root tkl with agglutination of t of ittakala (Brockelmann 1928:823). See aslo Kaufman 106. ETHIOPIC: Te. täklä 'plant, pitch (a tent)', Tna. täkälä, Amh.Gur. täkälä, Arg. täkälä, Har. čēxāla 'build'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. takal 'plant', Kham. tikel.

meaning: 'make amends, pay compensa tion, replace'; 'takla 'in place of, in recompense for'; 'perhaps a development of meaning of takala I 'set up, establish, fix'.
origin_explanation: None

ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. täk*lä 'jackal', Te. takla; per haps from Cushitic: Bil. tägila, Qua. tax*ola, Sa.Af. täkla (so also Brockelmann 1950:25). The connec tion with Heb. šuʾāl 'fox', Syr. taʾlā suggested by Conti Rossini 1923:468 is unlikely.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. täklu.
None
None
SEMITIC: Ar. kutrān 'bedbug', Mh. kettōn. ETHIOPIC: Te. takan, Tna. taq*an, Amh. taq*an, Arg. tuḥan, Gur. takan, Har. tuxān; also in Cushit ic: Or. tukāni, Bil. tukān, Aw. tak*ana, Qab. tuḥāna.
probably misprint for tərīr (see ʾarara).
None

meaning: 'ignite, set on fire, burn'; 'probably from Amharic täkk 'äššä; in the other Ethiopian languages: Te. täkša 'cauterize', Tna.Amh. täk*äššä 'burn, brand cattle', Gur. täkššä 'light a fire'. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. täkas, Qua. täküs. See also tark*äššä'.
origin_explanation: None
ETHIOPIC: Amh.Arg. takāššā, Gur. takāšša.

meaning: 'menstruate, (T) be tired, become weary, (T) loaf, be idle'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. takkätä 'be tired'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. takkat 'ancient times' (from Geez).
see ta-kesa.

meaning: 'be sad, be grieved, be sorrowful, be distressed, be anxious, be troubled in mind, be concerned, show care'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: The connection with Ar. kazzā 'become dry, shrivel' with agglutinated t, suggested by Brockelmann 1908:533, Anm.3, is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. täkkäza 'be sad, be grieved'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. takaz.

ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. täkkäze 'river'; from the name of the river Täkkäze which forms part of the border between Eritrea and Tigre. However, it could also have meant originally 'river', coming to designate the river Täkkäze.
metathesis of tazkär (see zakara).

SEMITIC: Akk. tulā 'breast', connected with Ar. 'hill, elevation' (Dillmann 552, Holma 46). Buhl 879 and Koehler 1029 identify it with ill 'mound'. Ar. tall, Heb. tel, Aram. tillā, Syr. tellā. See talā.

M renders tallä, talā by 'intrepidity, steadi-ness'. He apparently misunderstood Amh. täqäqänna (the translation given by Tayyä for talā) as coming from täqäq 'änā 'be cruel, be intrepid', but in fact it means 'cut of meat from the ventral region' and is perhaps to be connected with tallä, above.

ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur. tälba 'flax'; also Te. tälbägar 'linen', Amh. yätälba ägär 'flax plant', lit. 'the foot of the flax'; perhaps from Cushitic: Qab.Or. talba, Aw. tilbi, Qem. tärba, Qua. terbä. Conti Rossini 1912:260 thinks that flax was intro-duced into Ethiopia from South Arabia.
see walada.
tologäwi, tewologäwi 'theologian'; see tewologos.
see lahayya.
(D 1396, T.M) 'tree-covered mountain'
talm (pl. talām, 'atläm, 'atlamī) 'furrow'.

SEMITIC: Ar. talam 'furrow' (for which see Fraenkel 131), Heb. telem, Aram. talmā, tälāmā, Ug. tlm. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. tälläma 'make furrows', tälläma; Te.Amh. talm 'furrow'; passed into Cushitic: Bil. täläm.
(T.M) 'magnificent, superb'

meaning: (D 1403, K.OT) 'wizard, idol in form of a snake'
origin_explanation: The Septuagint has theletēn in 4 Kings 21:6, and the form teläm is probably an approximate transcription of the Greek noun.

From Ar. tilmīd, of Aramaic origin (Fraenkel 254) also in Tna. tälmədän 'apprentice'. According to von Soden 1311 it is Akk. talmīdu which developed into Heb. talmīd, Aram. talmīdā, Ar. tilmīd. See also Wagner 119, Kaufman 107.

meaning: (D 1403), place-name meaning 'pavement', but the printed text of the Paralipomena published by S. Grébaut has golasroṭän ተለስሮጠን. See litostros.
origin_explanation: See litostros.
seems to be a corrupt form of litostros, above.
see talawa, talwat.

meaning: 'follow, come behind, succeed, accompany, cling to, adhere, stay close to'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. talā (tlw) 'follow', SAr. tlw 'continue to do something'. It is considered to be connected with Semitic tly 'hang' (Heb. tālā, Aram. talā, Akk. talā) by Hommel 57 who translates Sem. tly with an original meaning 'hang on, fasten to, append > follow'. This connection is also suggested by Koehler 1029 and von Soden 1369. ETHIOPIC: Te. tälä (tlw) 'follow', Tna. täläwä, Amh. tälä, tolo 'fast'. Te. also täläw belo 'become friends'. For G. talot, tälwat 'wedding gift', cp. Amh. talot, tälwat 'dowry', Te. mätlo (from tälä 'give a dowry'), Gur. talot 'servant given by parents to their newly married daughter', that is, 'the one that follows the bride'. For a similar development, cp. Ar. tābi 'servant', from tabia 'follow'.
see tmm.
distorted form of Gk. thymiama 'incense, fragrant stuff' (W. Müller); also in Syr. tummā, tūmīs.
from Ar. tūm, tūm (for which see Fraenkel 58); also Te. tom, Har. tumma, Gur. tuma; Cushitic: Kam. tuma, Som. ton-ii. See also the doublet sim.
see ma'aka.
see m.
(HG) 'kind of tree'
SEMITIC: Heb. təmōl, 'etmōl, Aram. 'itmāl, Syr. tamāl, 'etmālī, Md. 'itmāl, Akk. umāli/u. Note the
ETHIOPIC: Tna. timma bäla 'roar', Amh. tämmamma, timm alä.
from Ar. tamām 'complete'; also Te. tämmä 'be complete', Tna. tämämä, Amh. tämam 'the right amount'.
SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. tinnīn 'enormous snake, sea monster' (for which see Fraenkel 123), Heb. tannīn, Aram.-Syr. tannīnā (Dillmann 555), Ug. tnn 'dragon', Md. tanina. ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. tämän.

phonetics: temän, teman I ኀመ 'south wind, (T) east';
origin_explanation: from Gk. thaiman < Heb. temän 'south, southern quarter'.
see also 'atämen.
probably from Ar. tamr 'dates'; also in Te. tämär, Tna. tämri, Amh.Gur. tämär, Arg. tämär. SEMITIC: Sar. tmr, Soq. timreh, Heb. tämär, Aram. tamrā, Syr. tamrṭā, Md. tumarta.

meaning: 'box, chest, kind of wood, ward-robe, (T) precious stone';
origin_explanation: from Gk. thema 'coffer' and ergab (I Kings 6:15 = I Sam. 6:15) which is a wrong transcription of Heb. 'argäz 'saddlebag'.
from Heb. tammūz, the Hebrew fourth month. also tamuz.
J. Halévy, JA 9 (1867). 385 interprets it as being altered from Heb. ḥammā 'sun' by changing ḥ (አ) to t (ኀ).
ETHIOPIC: Te. tumat 'haft of spear', Amh. tumaṭa 'spear with which one hits without throwing it', Gur. toma 'haft of spear'; from Cushitic: Or. tumäte 'spear', Som. tumaṭi, Qab.Kam. tōma.

phonetics: tamuz, tämūz ኀሙዝ ኀሙዝ, (T) tämūs ኀሙስ
meaning: Hebrew-Syriac name of the month tammūz.

phonetics: ten, tin ኀን, ኀን, (K) tan ኀን, (KG) tenä ኀነ
origin_explanation: see also tenqom 1.
from Ar. tumbāk 'tobacco'; also Te. tambak, tamba-ko, tanbak, Tna. tambako, Amh. tambako, tambaka, Har. timbäxo, Gur. tumbakko; in Cushitic: Or. timbō, Had. tambō-a, Bil. tinbawk, Sa. timbako.

meaning: 'intercede, act as an intercessor, be an ambassador';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. nbl 'dispatch', nblt, tbll 'diplomatic mission'. Dillmann 562 analyzes G. tanbala as being composed of the prefix tan- and bl from bhl 'say' so also Albright, RA 16[1919].186 who explains tanba-la as coming from tabbala < tabhala). Brockelmann 1908:233 (following Praetorius) derives it from wbl: Heb. hōbīl, Aram. hēbīl 'bring, send', with w > n before b, the root becoming nbl in South Arabic. See also Noeldeke 1910:198. ETHIOPIC: Tna. tänbälä 'intercede', Amh. tänbäbälä 'intercede', tänbälat 'Muslim' (from Geez). probably also Te. tänbäla 'make a gift to a newly-married person'.

ETHIOPIC: Amh. tänäffägä 'smell bad'. While it is possible that it may go back to nfg with a prefix ta-, as recorded by K 664 for Geez and by DTW for Amharic, it became a quadriradical in Geez and in Amharic. K 664 connects the Geez root nfg with fagaga I 'have a bad odor'.
it has the same meaning as täntäg (below) and one wonders whether tanfäg is not a misprint instead of täntäg (M copied Tayyä).

meaning: 'drops of water, wetness of the ground, vapor'.

origin_explanation: 'astanfasa 'cause to breathe, inhale, smell, blow into, sniff'; mastanfäs 'respiration'; ETHIOPIC: Amh. tänäffäsä 'breathe', Arg. tänäffäsä, Gur. tänäffäsä. It is a secondary quadriradical by agglutination of the morpheme tä- to the root nfs (see Cohen 1939:251-2).
a secondary formation of tanḥawa; see nḥw.

phonetics: tänäk ኀነክ (K), tenäk ኀነክ (D.K.T), tanak, tanakä ኀነክ ኀነከ (T), tänäq ኀነቅ
meaning: 'a plant whose root is used for the fermentation of beer';
origin_explanation: it is rendered in Amharic by šäddo, täddo (for which see Strelcyn 1973:no. 185). and also by wärka 'sycamore'.

it occurs in a text by Cosmas Indicopleustes in connection with the Agau, "then the natives arrive, bringing with them gold in the form of pea-sized nuggets called tankharas" (Y.M. Kobishchanov, Axum, 1979, p. 178). Since the word is not Greek, it is most probably of Agau origin. Note that Ar. tinkār 'sodium borate' (Dillmann 564) is only acci-dentally similar.
Dillmann 1907:136 derives it from ktm 'cover' and translates tankatam literally 'covering of the river'.

'atnana (K*), causative; tann 'vapor, smoke'; SEMITIC: Aram.-Syr. tanānā 'smoke', Md. tana 'vapor, smoke' (from Syriac). If, however, the sug-gestion made by Brockelmann 1928:828 concerning Syr. tanānā as coming from ʿnn (Heb. ʿānān 'smoke', Ar. ʿann) is correct, the root of Syr.-Aram. cannot be compared with the Ethiopic root. Rabin, HS 92, no. 79 considers G. tann 'smoke' a Syriac loanword. ETHIOPIC: Te. tänna (tnn), (T) tänänna 'smoke' (v.), Amh. tänänna 'evaporate, vaporize', Gaf. tänna 'smoke' (n.), tänänna 'smoke' (v.), Gur. tänänna.

phonetics: tanäyä, tanäyiyä ኀነየ ኀነየየ (D 1430, K.T.M), tanäyiyä ኀነየየ (Lt)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. tanäniyya 'mosquito', Amh. tänänni, also in Cushitic: Kham. tinne, Qem. tenen.
qom in tenqom is the Amharic equivalent of ten (see ten, above), and it should be written ኀንቆም tenqom. Note that M copied the entry from Tayyä.

meaning: 'performance of a choral song in the church'
origin_explanation: Whereas M's source of tenqom I is Tayyā, tenqom II is not found anywhere else except in M.

meaning: 'rise from the dead, rise up, rise against, set out, be set up'
origin_explanation: denominative from tanṣāʾe, for which see naṣʾa.

ETHIOPIC: Tna. tāntāgā 'poke the fire', tontag 'firebrand', Amh. tāntāgā, tāttāgā 'scorch, burn with hot coal', tontag 'firebrand'; also in Cushitic: Kham. tītīg 'firebrand'. D and K derive tntg from a root ntg, but in view of Amh. tāntāgā, tāttāgā, and Tna. tāntāgā, the Geez root is likewise tntg. See also tanfāq.

meaning: 'be shaken, vacillate, totter, stumble, stagger, hesitate, be downhearted, reel, be stirred, be alarmed, be seized with anguish'
origin_explanation: `atantana, causative; also 'stupefy'; tantun, participial meaning of tantana; tantāne 'vacillation, hesitation, staggering, perplexity, trepidation'; tantāno, same; SEMITIC: related to Ar. taltala, tartara 'shake, agitate', with alternance of liquids. ETHIOPIC: Amh. tantātanā 'stumble, stagger'.

meaning: 'ponder, mull over a matter, be preoccupied with something assiduously'
origin_explanation: P. Haupt, ZDMG 69 (1915), 564 thinks that the root is ntn 'give' (astə- being a prefix) and he compares Heb. nātan 'give', Akk. nadānu. According to him the meaning 'sich eifrig mit etwas beschäftigen' goes back to 'sich hingeben'. Note that Rundgren, OS 10 (1961), 112 also thinks, with reservations, of a root ntn. However, in view of Te. tāntāna 'take care (of a sick person)', astāntānā 'nurse', and Amh. tantātanā 'analyze', astāntānā 'ponder', the root of G. `astantana is tntn with the prefix 'as-'.
tepa (T) 'give shade' (denominative).
[Note that M apparently misunderstood dəbāb 'umbrella' (Tayyā's translation of as being dubba 'pumpkin' and thus wrongly rendered ṭāpi by 'pumpkin'].
from Gk. topazion 'topaz'; also in Tna. topazyon, Amh. topazyon, topazyon.

SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. taqaba 'penetrate', taqab 'perforation, hollow' (that is, the sheath is considered hollow, or as something into which the sword penetrates), even though the correspondence t:t is not normal.
From Ar. tiqda, taqda 'coriander seed'.
teqel ṭaqala (T) 'scales (for weighing)'; it refers symbolically to the Gospel era; from Gk. thekel, transcription of Aram. teqel 'unit of weight'; also Amh. teqel (from Geez).
probably a misprint (or mistake) for taqāhawa (see qahawa).
see qwm.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. tāqassala, tāqāttala, same; from (tā)qāttalā 'be attached'; see qṣl II.
it comes close to torā I.

(see Ludolf 203); ETHIOPIC: Tna. Amh. torā 'large antelope'. The identification with Te. wāhr 'bull', with initial t, suggested by Hommel 390, is possible if one considers also tayfan, below. See also Hommel 252, 380.

meaning: 'lie on a cushion, recline on a cushion, support one's head with a headrest'

origin_explanation: From Ar. turāb 'dust, powdery earth, remains, ashes'; also Amh. turāb 'dust, ashes of burnt incense'.
Amh. k°ərənāt
metathesis of tabriz; see brz I.

meaning: 'be left, be left behind, be left over, be abandoned, remain, survive, be spared, be in plenty, abound, be in excess, be superfluous, be excellent, be distinguished'; (ṭarfa omhawiro 'he gave up walking'); (zayaṭarraf 'more');
origin_explanation: atrafa, caus. of ṭarfa; also 'leave behind, abandon, save, spare, reserve, keep something for someone, have too much'; ṭarfi, act. part. of ṭarfa; also 'survivor'; ṭaruf 'left, remaining, survivor, superfluous, abundant, eminent, excellent, distinguished, outstanding, perfect, virtuous'; ṭarəft (fem.), ṭarufāt (pl.) 'virtue, perfection, excellence, work of excellence, virtuous deeds'; ṭarf 'remainder, remnant, abundance, abundant, superfluous, uttermost, surplus'; (ṭarfa nagar za-Bārok 'Paralipomena [omissions] of Baruch'); ṭarafāt (Lt) 'surplus, barns, stores (in which the surplus is put), Paralipomena'; ṭaraf (Lt) 'interest on a loan';

meaning: 'whatever is near the head or at the head (pillow, canopy)'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: the Geez verb as well as that of the Ethiopian languages is a denominative of ṭəras 'pillow' for which see rʾs; also Tna. ṭərasā 'a cushion', mātāras 'headrest, pillow'; Te. 'lay the head-support under the head', mātāras 'pillow'; Amh. (tān)tārasā 'put a pillow under the head', təras 'headrest'; Gur. tārāsa 'put a pillow under the head', təras, mātāras 'pillow'; Har. matras. The root passed into Coptic: Sa. mataras 'pillow', Be. metaras.

meaning: 'remainder, rest, residue, reserves, abundance, surplus, overflow, excellence'
origin_explanation: ṭarrāfā nagast 'the Book of the Chronicles' (lit. 'the particulars left out from the Book of Kings'); matraf, part. of `atrafa; SEMITIC: Ar. tarīfa 'live in opulence', taraf 'luxury, opulence', Sar. trf 'remain', Soq. tərəf 'be in good health'; ETHIOPIC: Te. tərfa 'be left over, remain', Tna. Gur. ṭārāfā, Amh. ṭārāfā, Arg. ṭārāfā, Har. ṭārāfā.
From Gk. theraphin, transcription of Heb. tərāfīm 'idols'. See also tərāfən II.
although the connection is not very clear it is perhaps to be identified with tərāfən I.
seems to be connected with rag°a.
see ragada.

`atarg°ama, causative; targ°āmī 'interpreter, commentator'; targ°um 'translated, expounded'; targ°āme 'interpretation, translation, commentary, exegesis, exposition'; targ°əmt 'interpretation'; targ°āmān 'interpreter'; targ°əmnā 'interpretation'; `astarg°āmī 'interpreter'; matarg°ām 'interpreter, commentator, translator'; of Aramaic origin (Noeldeke 1910:39) < Hebraic-Jewish origin (Ullendorff 1968:123); also in Te. tärg°ama 'interpret, explain', Tna. tärg°āmā, Amh. tärg°āmā, Gur. tärg°ām 'preaching'; SEMITIC: Ar. targamān 'translator', PBh. tirgem 'translate' (denominative), Aram. targəmānā 'transla

meaning: Akk. ta/urgumânu 'interpreter' (see Zimmern 7). On the Hittite origin of this root, see Salonen 12, Rabin, Orientalia 32 (1963).134-6. See also I. Gelb, "The word for Dragoman in the Ancient Near East", Glossa 2 (1968).93-104; Greenspahn 168.

origin_explanation: From Ar. tarikh 'date, history, chronicle, annals' (for its origin from South Arabic wrh 'moon', see F. Rosenthal, A history of Muslim historiography, 1957, pp. 11-13); also in Tna.Amh.Gur. tarik 'histo-ry', Har. tawrix, tariy 'calendar'.

meaning: 1) = Amh. 'aqqattala 'burn, set on fire'; 2) = Amh. täräkk'asa 'snuff (a candle), extinguish (a torch)';
origin_explanation: For the meaning 'burn, set on fire' the root is to be compared with takk'asa (above), with augmented r.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. täromi 'kind of tree', Amh. tärmi 'tree of the lowland whose wood is used to make the framework of a house'. See also tarmin.

meaning: 'kind of tree' (rendered in Amharic by dädäho and kätkatta, for which see Strelcyn 1973; no. 66, 139);
origin_explanation: transcription of Gk. rhathmón, from Heb. rotem 'broom-shrub' (Ludolf 260). See almso tarme.
from Gk. thermos 'lupine, wolf bean'; also in Ar. tarmus (for which see Lane I.146), Syr. tärmus, Amh. tärmus.

meaning: 'be numerous, multiply, abound' (the form is strange);
origin_explanation: trropi (T.M), tärop (T) 'numerous'; Guidi 1906-921 states that "almeno apparentemen-te, sembrerebbe essere = trop", that is, French trop (tro) pronounced trop (with p) by Aläqa Tayyä. Could it be rather Italian troppo? Grimme, ZDMG 68 (1914). 264 connects it with Ar. taraba which he translates 'be numerous', but in fact Ar. taraba means 'abound with dust'. There is also the possibili-ty of identifying it with G. tarfa 'be in plenty'. See also trp II.

atrapa (MA), same; täräp (T), 'aträpi (MA) 'trap for a snare'; probably from French trappe 'snare'. This would then be a late Geez word invented by someone with a knowledge of French. For the possibility of a French loanword, see also trp I.
Amharic has tärsu 'young elephant', p 'young' referring also to another animal.
from Gk. tharseis, transcription of Heb. tarsis. Amh. tärses (from Geez), Syr. tarsis.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. tärräwä 'arrive, come'; Geez wa would then mean 'make arrive'.
from Ar. turayyä, for which see Kunitzsch 1959.55, no. 19. See also taräw, below.

meaning: 'make nine, become nine' (denominative); tas'u, tas'u (used with a feminine noun) 'nine'; tas'attu, tas'attu (used with a mascu-line noun) 'nine'; tas'e 'nine' (with fem. noun); tas'a 'nine times'; täs'a 'ninth'; tas'u 'nine days, the ninth day (of a month)'; tassu' (M) 'nine times'; tas'u'at (with or without sa'at 'hour') 'nine o'clock, ninth hour'; tas'a, tas'a 'ninety';

origin_explanation: tas'äwi (Gr) 'the union of nine'; täs'äwi, täs'äwy 'ninth'; matassä' (K*), part. of tassä'a; mätas'it (M) 'ninefold'; SEMITIC: Ar. tis' 'nine', SAr. ts', Soq. se'eh ts'), Heb. tes'a, Aram.-Syr. tos'a, Md. tsa, Ug. Akk. tis'e. ETHIOPIC: Te. täs'a 'ninth', sä' 'nine', Tna. täs'atä, Amh. täsiyat 'afternoon, period from fifth to the ninth hour of the day' (according to Ethiopian count), that is, from eleven to three P.M.
seems to be from a root sgd with the prefix t-.

tatosha, passive; tatawäsäha 'be mingled with each other'; tosähi (K*), act. part. of tosha; tusuh 'mixed, blended'; tusähe (pl. tusäheyät) 'mixing, (Lt) temper, confusion'; tusäht 'mixing, mingling, composition, temper'; SEMITIC: Ar. wasiha 'be dirty', wittasaha, with agglu-nated t in Geez (Noeldeke 1910:187). ETHIOPIC: Gur. tosa 'be dirty, be confused', t-analyzed as t-wsh, and perhaps to be compared with Tna. hawwäsä 'mix' (with metathesis), passed to Cushitic. Kam.Had. tosa 'be dirty'.

meaning: name of a Jewish and Syrian month;
origin_explanation: from Heb. tisri, the seventh month, Syr. tesrin.
corruption of Gk. heksamitos 'of six strands'.

meaning: 'colic because of a tapeworm, (M) uterine tumor'.

origin_explanation: variant of tawi (see below); Amh. tito 'annual cycle, completion of the yearly round'.
from Ar. tut (of Aramaic origin; see Fraenkel 140); also Amh. tut. In the other Semitic languages: PbH. tut, Aram.-Syr. tujä (see Löw 395, no. 338).
from a root thth, and cp. Tna. tahtah bäla 'perspire'.
totana (T.M) 'tie the shoelaces' (denominative); ETHIOPIC: Amh. totan 'shoelace', from tawata 'tie (shoelaces), interlace' (see below), the noun totän being formed with the suffix -n.

meaning: 'decrees, Orders of the Apostles, titles (applying to the books of the Bible)';
origin_explanation: a corrupt rendering of Gk. titlos 'title, section, chapter in conciliar documents' (from Latin titulus 'section, chapter, passage in a book'); also in Amh. täjläsat, Ar. tatläsät and titlis. The Greek titlos is also rendered in Geez in the form 'abțlis (see above).
it is difficult to know whether the root is tațara or țarra with the prefix ta, that is, tațarra.
from Coptic tôbe, tôbi; also in Ar. țuba.
from Gk. theologos 'theologian'; also Tna. tewologiya 'theology', Amh. torologiya. See also tologannã.
from Gk. theologos 'theologian'; also Tna. tewologiya 'theology', Amh. torologiya. See also tologannã.
from Gk. theologos 'theologian'; also Tna. tewologiya 'theology', Amh. torologiya. See also tologannã.
from Gk. theologos 'theologian'; also Tna. tewologiya 'theology', Amh. torologiya. See also tologannã.

meaning: 'season of the small rains (April-July), spring rain, (Gr.201) early rain'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Gordon 49 compares, with reservations, Ug. tant 'the rain'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. tãwãn 'the small rains', Amh. tãwãn, in zãmãnã tãwãn 'rain that falls in the dry season'; also in Cushitic: Bil. tunãn.

SEMITIC: Gordon 49 compares, with reservations, Ug. tant 'the rain'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. tãwãn 'the small rains', Amh. tãwãn, in zãmãnã tãwãn 'rain that falls in the dry season'; also in Cushitic: Bil. tunãn.
Latin tunica; for its connection with Gk. khitõn coming from Semitic, see Masson 27-9, Baumgartner 481.
from Ar. tuniyya < Gk othonion 'linen cloth'. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil.Qua.Qem. tãwinã 'dress, tunic', undoubtedly taken from Arabic.
from Ar. tawr.
from Ar. tawãsim, pl. of tãsũma 'shoe', for which see Dozy 1845:104.
it is the root of totãn (see above), from twtw, and cp. Te. tawã (twy) 'turn, wind', related to twy (see tawaya, below).
from Coptic thout, thaut; also Ar. (Eg) tot. See also tito.
from Gk. theia, nom.-acc. pl. of theion 'sulfur'; also in Syr. tayyũn, pl. tayã.

SEMITIC: Dillmann 571 (followed by Hommel 264, 286) connects it with Ar. yafan 'young bull', with prefixed t agglutinated to the root. ETHIOPIC: Tna. tãfîn, tefãn, tefãnti, tãfãnti 'young bullock', Amh. Arg. wãyfãn, wefãn, Gur. wãfen; also in Cushitic: wufani. See also torã.

meaning: 'go uninvited from house to house, eat at the expense of others'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. tãha (tyh) 'behave insolently' (Lane). ETHIOPIC: related to Te. tawha (twhy) 'wander about'. See also tayyəha.
SEMITIC: Ar. tãha (tyh) 'behave insolently' (Lane). ETHIOPIC: related to Te. tawha (twhy) 'wander about'. See also tayyəha.

meaning: 'the going around uninvited from house to house'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. tãha (tyh) 'behave insolently' (Lane). ETHIOPIC: related to Te. tawha (twhy) 'wander about'. See also tayyəha.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. tiha, teha 'torch'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. tiha, teha 'torch'.

Dillmann 571 renders tayyãhi by 'terrified (wandering about)'; in this case it could be identified with teha I. SEMITIC: Ar. tãha (twh) 'be perplexed, wander about, get lost', Aram.-Syr. tawah 'be alarmed, be startled'.

Dillmann 571 renders tayyãhi by 'terrified (wandering about)'; in this case it could be identified with teha I. SEMITIC: Ar. tãha (twh) 'be perplexed, wander about, get lost', Aram.-Syr. tawah 'be alarmed, be startled'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. tãyãhi (Geez).
from Gk. theatron 'theater'; also Tna. tayatros (from Geez), tayãtər (from French), Amh. tãyãtər (from French).
from Gk. theatron 'theater'; also Tna. tayatros (from Geez), tayãtər (from French), Amh. tãyãtər (from French).
from Gk. theatron 'theater'; also Tna. tayatros (from Geez), tayãtər (from French), Amh. tãyãtər (from French).
